planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or divide established plants in spring or fall; spiked speedwell transplants easily and blooms reliably in its first or second year from seed. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage reblooming.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; spiked speedwell is one of the most reliably beautiful summer perennials in the European tradition, producing dense, tapering spikes of tiny flowers in intense shades of violet-blue, lilac, or white (depending on cultivar) that bloom from midsummer into fall. The plant is extremely tough and drought tolerant once established, thriving in lean, well-drained soil where it is long-lived, but declining in rich, moist conditions where it becomes floppy and short-lived. Popular cultivars include 'Royal Candles' (deep violet, compact), 'Icicle' (white), 'Red Fox' (deep pink), and 'Heidekind' (rose-pink). An excellent front-of-border perennial that combines beautifully with ornamental grasses, sedums, and rudbeckia in late-season gardens. Attracts numerous small butterflies and bumblebees.

planting.note: Direct sow outdoors in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or cold stratify seeds 4–6 weeks and sow indoors in late winter. Lady's mantle self-sows readily once established and typically perpetuates itself without attention.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; common lady's mantle is one of the most beloved cottage garden perennials, treasured for the exquisite way its rounded, softly pleated, velvety leaves catch and hold water droplets and dew in perfect spherical beads that roll around the leaf surface. The frothy sprays of tiny, chartreuse-yellow flowers in early summer are superb as a foil for pink and lavender flowers and effective cut fresh or dried. In traditional European herbal medicine, Alchemilla was used as a wound herb and for women's health conditions — the genus name references medieval alchemists who collected the dew from the leaves. Self-sows reliably to naturalize in cool, moist, shaded garden areas. Best in cool-summer climates; in the South, plant in shade and ensure consistent moisture.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after cold stratification; or cold stratify seeds 60 days and sow indoors in late winter. Canadian burnet prefers moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade or at moist meadow edges.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Canadian burnet is a magnificent tall native of wet meadows and moist openings, producing elegant, pinnate compound leaves and distinctive bottlebrush flower heads — pure white 'poker' shapes on tall, airy stems — in late summer and fall. The flowers are among the most important late-season pollen sources for native bees, attracting sweat bees, bumblebees, and specialist pollen foragers to their elongated catkin-like heads. In the garden, Canadian burnet provides distinctive architectural interest when paired with tall ornamental grasses and late asters in moist borders or rain gardens. Unlike European sanguisorbas often found in nurseries, this native species naturally colonizes moist, shaded bottomlands and moist meadow edges throughout eastern North America.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in acidic, peaty or lean, well-drained soil in partial shade; tormentil grows naturally on heathlands, acidic meadows, and open woodland edges throughout Europe and grows slowly from seed. Division of established clumps in spring or fall is more reliable for propagation.

directions: Plant in lean, acidic, well-drained to dry soil in partial shade to full sun; tormentil is a creeping, low-growing European perennial of heathlands and acidic meadows, recognized by its distinctive four-petaled yellow flowers (most Potentilla have five petals) and deeply divided leaves with 3–5 leaflets. The roots are exceptionally rich in tannins and astringent compounds — among the highest tannin content of any European herb — and have been used for centuries in traditional medicine as an anti-diarrheal remedy, wound herb, and tea substitute. The root was also historically used in leather tanning and as a red dye. In the garden, tormentil makes a charming low groundcover for dry, acidic, heather-garden conditions where few ornamental plants thrive. The yellow flowers bloom profusely from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; American bugleweed grows naturally along stream banks, wet meadow edges, and pond margins throughout North America. The plant spreads by rhizomes to form colonies in appropriate wet conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile to average soil in partial shade to full sun at pond edges, stream banks, and wet meadow margins; American bugleweed is a native mint-family herb of North American riparian habitats, closely related to European gypsywort (Lycopus europaeus). The plant has a long history of use in American herbal medicine, particularly for reducing elevated thyroid function (hyperthyroidism) — a traditional application supported by modern research showing that lycopus extracts inhibit thyroid hormone activity. The small, white tubular flowers are inconspicuous but attract numerous small native bees and hoverflies. The plant is valuable in rain gardens, bioswales, and wet garden edges where its preference for saturated soil makes it naturally suited. Note: persons with thyroid conditions should consult a healthcare provider before using medicinally.
